misconception: She's rude and doesn't want friends

chapters.title: Eye Contact
chapters.intro: Dana joins your group for a science activity. She doesn't look at anyone when she talks. She explains her idea while looking at the table.
chapters.question: What do you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell her 'look at us when you talk, it's rude'
choices.outcome: Dana goes quiet and stops explaining. Her idea — which was actually excellent — is lost. She shrinks in her chair and says nothing for the rest of the activity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assume she's being unfriendly and don't include her ideas
choices.outcome: Dana keeps talking, but no one engages. She assumes people don't like her ideas, not realising they've already decided not to listen. Her contribution disappears before it starts.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Listen to her idea — it's actually really good
choices.outcome: You listen carefully. Dana's idea is creative and detailed — exactly what the project needs. The group uses it and the work gets much better. Dana looks relieved.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ask the teacher if Dana can be in a different group
choices.outcome: The teacher says no. Dana overheard the request. The rest of the activity is quiet and uncomfortable for everyone — especially Dana.

facts: Avoiding eye contact is common in autism. It is not rude — eye contact can actually feel overwhelming and painful for autistic people.
Autistic people often communicate just as clearly without eye contact. Judging someone's friendliness by where they look can cause you to miss what they're actually saying.
chapters.reflection: Have you ever been so focused on something that you forgot to look up? What were you thinking about?
chapters.myth: Not looking at people means she's being rude
chapters.reality: Eye contact can feel overwhelming for autistic people — she listens better without it

chapters.title: The Noise
chapters.intro: There is a fire drill. The alarm is extremely loud. Dana covers her ears and looks very distressed. Other kids think she's being dramatic.
chapters.question: What do you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell her to calm down — it's just a drill
choices.outcome: Dana can't calm down on command when her senses are overwhelmed. Your words make her feel like she's failing at something she can't control. She covers her ears tighter and shakes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Say 'she always makes a scene' to your friend
choices.outcome: Dana hears you. She already feels like she doesn't belong. Your words confirm it. She avoids your table for weeks after.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stay near her and walk with her outside calmly
choices.outcome: You don't try to fix it — you just stay. Your calm presence helps. Dana gets outside and slowly recovers. She gives you a small nod later. That nod means a lot.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Get the teacher straight away and point at Dana
choices.outcome: The teacher helps, but pointing draws everyone's attention. Dana is surrounded by staring faces at the worst possible moment. It makes things harder, not easier.

facts: Many autistic people experience sensory sensitivities. Sounds, lights, or textures that are easy for others can be genuinely painful for them.
Sensory overload is a real neurological response, not a performance. The most helpful thing is often simply staying calm and not adding more stimulation.
chapters.reflection: Think of something that bothers you that doesn't bother others — a smell, a texture, a sound. How would you feel if people told you to just ignore it?
chapters.myth: She's being dramatic about a little noise
chapters.reality: Sensory overload is real neurological pain — not a performance

chapters.title: The Routine Change
chapters.intro: A substitute teacher changes the usual seating plan. Dana becomes very upset and refuses to move. The substitute gets frustrated. Other students watch.
chapters.question: What do you do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tell the sub 'she does this all the time, ignore her'
choices.outcome: Dana hears you dismiss her. The substitute ignores her distress. The situation escalates, and Dana ends up spending the rest of the class in the hallway — alone.

choices.label: B
choices.outcome: Nothing gets better by watching. Dana becomes more distressed and the substitute more frustrated. It turns into a confrontation that neither of them wanted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Quietly explain to the sub that Dana needs her usual seat
choices.outcome: The substitute understands and lets Dana take her usual spot. Dana settles almost immediately. Class continues. You just de-escalated a situation before it became serious.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tell Dana to just sit anywhere — it doesn't matter
choices.outcome: To Dana, it matters enormously. Your words feel like no one understands her. She shuts down for the rest of the day.

facts: Predictable routines provide safety and reduce anxiety for autistic people. Unexpected changes can feel genuinely overwhelming — not dramatic.
Autistic people are often described as 'inflexible', but what looks like stubbornness is usually a coping strategy. The routine isn't a preference — it's a regulation tool.
chapters.reflection: Is there something in your day that, if it suddenly changed, would really throw you off? What would help in that moment?
chapters.myth: She's stubborn about her seat for no reason
chapters.reality: Routines are how Dana keeps the world predictable and safe — change feels overwhelming, not optional
